blocks.text: Chapter 3.
Why You Can't Do Probability

Third night, friend, and tonight I must tell you about a missing organ. You have an eye for light, an ear for sound, a tongue for sweetness — and for probability, the mathematics of maybe... nothing. Evolution never built the organ. Your ancestors needed "tiger or no tiger," not "tiger, twelve percent."

So the brain fakes it — with stories, with feelings, with a sense of cosmic fairness. And tonight we watch the fakery fail, starting with one glorious summer night in Monte Carlo when it emptied a casino floor's worth of wallets all at once.
The night black came up twenty-six times

Monte Carlo, summer of 1913. The roulette ball lands on black. Then black again. And again. Five blacks. Ten. Fifteen. Word spreads through the casino like fire — come see the table where the wheel has gone mad. And as the streak stretches toward twenty-six blacks in a row, gamblers stampede... to bet on red. More and more with every spin, doubling and tripling their stakes. The wheel owes the world a red. Surely, surely now.

The casino made millions that night. Because the twenty-seventh spin — like every spin — was a fresh 50/50, and the ball, friend, has no memory. That is the gambler's fallacy: the certainty that random deviations must balance out soon, as if chance kept a ledger of debts. Flip a fair coin fifty heads in a row and the fifty-first is still exactly 50/50 — most people feel tails is due, importing justice into a process that has none. The same ghost whispers to lottery players avoiding last week's numbers, to parents of three daughters certain a son is "due," to investors sure a stock that fell five straight days must bounce. The one real skill here: ask whether events are independent. Cards drawn from a deck do change the odds; dice, wheels, and coins start the universe over every single time. When events are independent, the past tells you nothing — and any feeling of "due" is your brain inventing fairness where only chance lives.
Markus and the ten thousand truck drivers
Now meet Markus: a slim German man, wears glasses, loves Mozart. Question: is he more likely (a) a truck driver, or (b) a professor of literature in Frankfurt?

Nearly everyone says professor — the description fits so well. And nearly everyone is wrong, because Germany has roughly ten thousand times more truck drivers than Frankfurt literature professors. Even if only a sliver of truckers love Mozart, that sliver outnumbers all the professors put together. That is base-rate neglect: a vivid particular hijacks the judgment, and the boring background statistics — the base rates that should dominate — vanish from view. Medical schools drill against it with a maxim worth stealing: when you hear hoofbeats, think horses, not zebras. The startup with the brilliant pitch triggers your "next Google" template while the base rate — most startups fail — sits unconsulted. Dobelli's discipline is mechanical and humbling: anchor on the reference class first — what usually happens to things like this? — and let the compelling specifics be a small correction. Your intuition runs it exactly backward.
Linda the bank teller

Now, friend, the most famous trap in all of psychology — walk into it with your eyes open. Linda is 31, single, outspoken, a former philosophy student, deeply concerned with discrimination and social justice.
Pause and guess: Which is more probable: (a) Linda is a bank teller, or (b) Linda is a bank teller AND active in the feminist movement?
Ready? Here is what happened.

Around 85% of people choose (b) — and (b) is logically impossible to be more probable. Every feminist bank teller is also a bank teller; a subset can never outgrow the set that contains it. This is Tversky and Kahneman's conjunction fallacy: every detail you add to a story can only shrink its probability — but detail makes the story more plausible, and the brain happily pays plausibility prices for probability goods. It even fools experts: professional forecasters rated "oil consumption will drop 30%" as less likely than "a dramatic price rise will cause consumption to drop 30%" — a strict special case judged more probable than its parent, because the causal story felt so good. The warning for every pitch, forecast, and news analysis that wins you over with rich, connected detail: each vivid specific is one more condition that must also be true. A beautifully detailed scenario is improbable by construction. Strip the story to the bare event, and ask about that.
The shock and the whisper

Researchers once wired people up, told them an electric shock was coming, and measured their dread. The eerie finding: the body braced according to how big the shock would be — and hardly at all according to how likely it was. Near-certain or one-in-a-hundred, same sweat.

That is the neglect of probability: magnitude screams, probability whispers. It's the whole business model of lotteries — the marketing is jackpot size, and nobody's excitement drops tenfold when the odds fall from one in ten million to one in a hundred million, though the ticket just became ten times worse. It's why spectacular risks — plane crashes, terrorism — terrify wildly beyond their tiny probabilities while the statistically deadlier drive to the airport barely registers. And it hides a cousin: a risk falling from 5% to 0% feels far more precious than 10% to 5% — same five points, but only zero silences the alarm. Since your gut will never feel the difference between 0.001% and 1%, the only defense is paper: write the probability down, multiply it by the outcome, and let the arithmetic overrule the scream.
The paper that reaches for the sun
One more missing sense — and this one bankrupts kings.
Pause and guess: Take a sheet of paper. Fold it in half. Fold again — fifty folds in all. How thick is the stack? Trust your gut before you reveal.
Ready? Here is what happened.

About one hundred million kilometers — two-thirds of the way from the Earth to the sun. The gut says "a phone book," and the gut is off by a factor of billions, because each fold doubles, and doubling is exponential growth — a thing the human mind simply cannot feel. Evolution tuned us for linear life: twice the effort, twice the berries. So percentages sneak past us sounding harmless. The tool that restores sight is the rule of 70: divide 70 by the growth rate to get the doubling time. "Mild" 5% inflation? 70 ÷ 5 = 14 — prices double every fourteen years; a young adult watches money quarter by middle age. Accidents rising 7% a year means a doubling every decade. And the old Persian tale knew it all along: the sage who asked the king for rice — one grain on the first chessboard square, doubled on each square after — bankrupted the kingdom before square sixty-four. Never trust your gut on percentage growth. Divide 70 by X first.
Three quick ghosts

Three smaller ghosts haunt the same missing organ, friend — name them and they shrink. The clustering illusion: your pattern-hungry brain finds faces on Mars, "support levels" in stock charts, hot hands in basketball — but genuinely random sequences contain runs and clumps; a pattern proves nothing until statistics say otherwise. Regression to the mean: extremes drift back toward average all by themselves, because luck helped make them extreme — Israeli flight instructors concluded that praise ruined cadets and shouting improved them, when brilliant landings were simply followed by ordinary ones either way; the same illusion crowns doctors when fluctuating back pain "responds" to treatment, and fires the coach after a title season. Where luck plays any role, expect the drift — and refuse to invent a cause for it. And ambiguity aversion, Ellsberg's paradox: offered an urn with a known 50/50 mix or one with unknown proportions, people flee the unknown urn even when it can't be worse on average. We tolerate risk (known odds) far better than uncertainty (unknown odds) — yet careers, markets, and love offer only the second kind. An instinct that flees ambiguity steers you away from precisely the decisions that matter most.
Before you go

So the wheel keeps no ledger, the story shrinks as it beautifies, the shock's size drowns its odds, and the folded paper touches the sun. Where numbers wear uncertainty, your gut is not a witness — it is a novelist. Put the pen in arithmetic's hand instead.

But tomorrow, friend... tomorrow the traps stop being about lines and coins and wheels. Tomorrow they are about the person you see in the mirror — the smartest, most honest, hardest-working, above-average driver you know. Why everything you're good at feels like skill and everything that fails feels like bad luck. Why we swim daily in survivorship's warm lie. The self-deception chapter is the one Dobelli says runs under all the others.
Next time: how you fool the one person you can never escape.
Key takeaways
The ball has no memory: for independent events, streaks mean nothing and "due" is invented justice.
Anchor on base rates first — hoofbeats mean horses; let vivid specifics be only a small correction.
Conjunction fallacy: every added detail lowers probability while raising plausibility — strip stories to the bare event.
Magnitude screams, probability whispers: write odds down and multiply, because your gut can't feel the difference.
Exponential growth must be calculated, never felt — divide 70 by the growth rate for the doubling time.
Clusters in noise, extremes drifting to average, and fear of unknown odds are ghosts — demand statistics before crediting causes.
